title: Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
text:
To run this exercise, you need to have the following tools and libraries installed:

R and RStudio from CRAN and Posit from Posit
Python 3.x
Docker: install Docker Desktop for your operating system
MongoDB Compass: install the MongoDB GUI client
a dump of the MongoDB example database (file mongo_massm_2026-03-20.archive.gz) from the GDrive shared folder (see Moodle page)

Then you need to start a MongoDB instance using Docker:
mkdir ~/MASSM
docker run -d -p 27017:27017 -v ${PWD}/db:/data/db --name massm-mongo mongo:latest
cat mongo_massm_2026-03-20.archive.gz | docker exec -i massm-mongo mongorestore --gzip --archive --drop
or, on Windows PowerShell:
mkdir $env:USERPROFILE\MASSM
docker run -d -p 27017:27017 -v "${PWD}\db":/data/db --name massm-mongo mongo:latest
Get-Content mongo_massm_2026-03-20.archive.gz -Raw | docker exec -i massm-mongo mongorestore --gzip --archive --drop
Now launch MongoDB Compass and connect to mongodb://localhost:27017. You should see a database named MASSM_1.

title: Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
text:
2.1 Queries on MongoDB Compass
Using MongoDB Compass, perform the following queries on the MASSM_1 database:

Using the query field and the query option Project, select 100 documents from the force collection starting 10 seconds after the first timestamp, and only show the message.timestamp and message.measurements.Fx fields.
In the Aggregation tab, create a pipeline to compute the mean and standard deviation of the microphone component for each document. Save the aggregation as a named View.







Tip



Liberally use the AI assistant: learn how to give it precise and clear instructions, and ask it to write the query for you.








Important



Aggregations are powerful tools that can reduce computational load on database clients that consume data for analytics.
Note that with the green save button you can save the aggregation in the collection, so that you can run it again later, or you can create a view. A view is a virtual collection that is not materialized in the database, but it can be queried as a normal read-only collection, and it will execute the aggregation pipeline on the fly. This is useful to create custom data transformations that can be reused in different contexts.


Once you have customized your data query or transformation, you can export the results as a CSV file, and then load it in R or Python for further analysis and visualization. This approach, though, is not ideal for large datasets (you are duplicating the data!), and it is better to use a database client library to query the database directly from your code, as we will see in the next exercises.

title: Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
text:
2.2 Queries from R
We start loading some packages:

library(tidyverse)
library(mongolite)
library(glue)
options(digits = 15)
options(digits.secs = 3)







Warning



If your R installation does not have the above packages, you can install them using the command install.packages() function.


Now, using the mongolite library, write R code to perform the same queries as above, and load the results in a data frame. Then, create a plot of the Fx component over time, and a histogram of the microphone component.
For example, we could run:

m <- mongo(collection = "acceleration", db = "MASSM_1", url = "mongodb://localhost:27017")
t0 <- "2011-01-01T01:07:34.780+00:00"
query <- glue(
'{{
  "message.timestamp": {{
    "$gte": {{"$date": "{t0}"}}
  }}
}}'
)
fields <- '{
  "_id": 0, 
  "message.timestamp": 1,
  "message.measurements.microphone": 1
}'
df <- m$find(query=query, fields = fields, limit=200) %>% as_tibble()
tibble(
  timestamp = df$message$timestamp,
  microphone = map_dbl(df$message$measurements$microphone, mean)
) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=timestamp, y=microphone)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_x_datetime(date_labels = "%M.%OS", date_breaks = "0.05 sec")














Tip



The query result is a rather complicate data frame, for its columns are nested lists. Try to use an aggregation of a saved View to make it simpler and more efficient to load in R.

title: Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
text:
2.3 Queries from Python
Using the pymongo library, write Python code to perform the same queries as above, and load the results in a Pandas data frame. Then, create a plot of the Fx component over time, and a histogram of the microphone component.
For example, we could run:

from pymongo import MongoClient
import pandas as pd
from datetime import datetime
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
client = MongoClient("mongodb://localhost:27017")
db = client["MASSM_1"]
collection = db["acceleration"]
t0 = datetime.fromisoformat("2011-01-01T01:07:34.780+00:00")
query = {
    "message.timestamp": {
        "$gte": t0
    }
}
fields = {
    "_id": 0,
    "message.timestamp": 1,
    "message.measurements.microphone": 1
}
cursor = collection.find(query, fields).limit(100)
df = pd.DataFrame(list(cursor))
df["microphone"] = df["message"].apply(lambda x: sum(x["measurements"]["microphone"]) / len(x["measurements"]["microphone"]))
df["timestamp"] = df["message"].apply(lambda x: x["timestamp"])
plt.plot(df["timestamp"], df["microphone"])
plt.xlabel("Time")
plt.ylabel("Microphone")
plt.show()














Tip



As said above for R, the query result is a rather complicate data frame, for its columns are nested lists. Try to use an aggregation of a saved View to make it simpler and more efficient to load in Python.

title: Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
text:
3.1 Install MADS
To setup the MADS framework, follow the instructions in the MADS documentation. You need to install the MADS library. Beside, you need the MongoDB installed and loaded with example data as described above.
Furthermore, you need Python or R/RStudio.

title: Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
text:
3.2 Configuring MADS
MADS is a multi-agent system for data streaming and processing. It is based on the concept of agents that can be configured to perform specific tasks, such as data acquisition, processing, and visualization.
MADS agents connect to a central broker that manages the communication between agents. Each agent can subscribe to specific topics to receive data, and publish data on other topics for other agents to consume.
MADS has a central configuration file, that is loaded by the broker and distributed to all agents. There is also a GUI called director that orchestrate the launch of the broker and the agents, and allows to monitor their status and communication.
We start by creating a configuration file for our MADS setup, mads.ini, and a director configuration file director.toml.
In your current working directory, create the file mads.ini with the following content:
[agents]
frontend_address = "tcp://localhost:9090"
backend_address = "tcp://localhost:9091"
settings_address = "tcp://localhost:9092"
timecode_fps = 25

[broker]
frontend_address = "tcp://*:9090"
backend_address = "tcp://*:9091"
settings_address = "tcp://*:9092"

[feedback]
sub_topic = [""]
print_width = 0
indent_width = 2

[replay]
db_uri = "mongodb://localhost:27017"
db_name = "MASSM_1"
collections = ["force", "acceleration"]
max_loop_duration_ms = 1000
repeat = true
Then create the file director.toml with the following content:
[director] 
sample_rate = 2.0 # optional, default=2.0: process metrics sampling period in seconds (fractions allowed)

[broker]
command = "mads broker"
enabled = true
relaunch = true
tty = true

[feedback]
command = "mads feedback"
after = "broker"
enabled = true

[replay]
command = "mads source replay"
after = "broker"
enabled = false

title: Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
text:
3.3 Running MADS
Just launch the director with the command:
mads director
In the GUI that opens, you should see the broker and the feedback agent running. You can enable the replay agent to start streaming data from MongoDB by clicking on the start button. You should see the data being printed in the feedback agent.






Tip



The director GUI is just a convenient way to launch and monitor the MADS agents. You can also launch the agents directly from the command line, using the commands specified in the director.toml file. For example, you can launch the broker with mads broker, and then launch the replay agent with mads source replay.

title: Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
section: 3.4 Consuming MADS data in R and Python
text:
3.4 Consuming MADS data in R and Python
MADS is designed to be flexible. You can create custom agents to consume the data streamed by MADS and perform specific tasks, such as data processing, visualization, or machine learning.
Among the different strategies and languages, you can design your agents by following these strategies:

Create a custom C++ plugin as detailed here, and then use the plugin in your MADS configuration. This is the most efficient way to create custom agents, but it requires C++ programming skills.
Use the MADS Python agent to create a custom agent in Python. Easier than the C++ plugin, does not require to define the agent logic and loop, but it is less efficient than the C++ plugin and has some limitation in flexibility.
Use the MADS R plugin to create a custom agent in R. Similar as the previous case, but for R language.
Use the MADS Python library to create a custom agent entirely in Python that connects to the MADS broker and subscribes to the relevant topics to receive data. Very flexible, but you have to manage the agent logic and loop yourself.







ImportantReal-time



Note that, unless you run the MADS agents on realtime operating systems (like RT-Linux), you cannot guarantee real-time performance, and you may experience some delay in the data streaming. However, MADS is designed to be efficient and to minimize the latency, so it can be used for near-real-time applications.

title: Multi-agent system for smart machining, part II
text:
3.5 Task
Try to create a custom agent in Python or R that subscribes to the forces or accelerations data streams and performs some simple processing, such as computing the mean and standard deviation of the measurements, and then publishes the statistic back to MADS for visualization in the feedback agent.






TipGraphical visualization in Rerun



The MADS rerunner is a powerful tool for visualizing data in real-time. You can use it to create custom visualizations of the data streamed by MADS, such as time series plots, ACF, FFT. It can consume data produced by any other MADS agent and provide a real-time chart or graphical representation of the data.

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
PCA: data matrix and centering


Let the feature matrix of a MVTS be \[
\mathbf{X} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{x}_1^T \\
\mathbf{x}_2^T \\
\vdots \\
\mathbf{x}_N^T
\end{bmatrix}
\in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}
\] where each row is a feature vector extracted at one time instant or from one time window statistics
In other words, uppercase \(\mathbf{X}\) collects all observations, while lowercase \(\mathbf{x}_t\) denotes the feature vector of the \(t\)-th observation

PCA starts from the centered matrix \(\mathbf{X}_c\): \[
\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}^T
\] with sample means vector: \[
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{t=1}^{N}\mathbf{x}_t
\]
If variables have different physical units, one usually standardizes them before PCA

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
PCA: covariance matrix and eigenproblem


The sample covariance matrix (\(p\times p\))is: \[
\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{N-1}\mathbf{X}_c^T \mathbf{X}_c
\]
PCA solves the eigenvalue problem: \[
\mathbf{S}\mathbf{p}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{p}_i,
\quad i = 1,\dots,p
\]

where:

\(\lambda_i\) is the variance explained by the \(i\)-th principal component
\(\mathbf{p}_i\) is the corresponding eigenvector, or loading vector

The indexes \(i\) are sorted so that: \[
\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \dots \ge \lambda_p \ge 0
\]








Note


The eigenvectors \(\mathbf{p}_i\) are orthogonal and form a new coordinate system in feature space, where the first axis captures the largest variance, the second axis captures the largest remaining variance, and so on.

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
PCA: scores and low-dimensional representation



The projection of the data onto the principal axes gives the scores matrix (\(N\times p\)): \[
\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{P}
\] that is, \[
\mathbf{T} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\mathbf{t}_1^T \\
\mathbf{t}_2^T \\
\vdots \\
\mathbf{t}_N^T
\end{bmatrix},
~~~
\mathbf{t}_t^T = \mathbf{x}_{c,t}^T \mathbf{P},~~~\mathbf{P} = [\mathbf{p}_1,\mathbf{p}_2,\dots,\mathbf{p}_p]
\]

Using only the first \(a < p\) components yields the reduced model \[
\mathbf{X}_c \approx \mathbf{T}_a \mathbf{P}_a^T
\] where: \[
\mathbf{T}_a = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{P}_a
\]









Note


For a given subset of principal components \(a\), the explained variance ratio is: \(\eta_a = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{a}\lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{p}\lambda_i}\). This can be used to select the number of components to retain, for example by setting a threshold such as \(\eta_a \ge 0.95\).





Note: We can use transpose rather than inverse because \(\mathbf{P}\) is an orthonormal matrix, so \(\mathbf{P}^{-1} = \mathbf{P}^T\).

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
section: PCA: reconstruction error and monitoring statistics
text:
PCA: reconstruction error and monitoring statistics



For centered observation row \(\mathbf{x}_{c,t}^T\), PCA computes the score row \[
\mathbf{t}_t^T = \mathbf{x}_{c,t}^T \mathbf{P}_a
\] and then reconstructs it in feature space as \[
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{c,t}^T = \mathbf{t}_t^T \mathbf{P}_a^T
\]
Therefore the residual decomposition is: \[
\mathbf{x}_{c,t}^T = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{c,t}^T + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t^T
\] where \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{c,t}^T\) lies in the retained latent subspace and \(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_t^T\) is the residual row

Two standard monitoring statistics are:

Hotelling’s \(T^2\) : \[
T_t^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\frac{t_{ti}^2}{\lambda_i}
\]
Squared Prediction Error, SPE or \(Q\) statistic: \[
Q_t = \|\mathbf{\varepsilon}_t\|_2^2
  = \sum_{j=1}^{p}\varepsilon_{tj}^2
\]










Note


\(T^2\) measures abnormality inside the retained subspace, while \(Q_t\) measures lack of fit outside it

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
section: PCA: why it is useful for time series
text:
PCA: why it is useful for time series
PCA is applied to time-series analysis after building features from samples or windows
Typical uses are:

reducing a multivariate signal to a few latent variables
detecting operating regimes and structural changes
decorrelating strongly coupled measurements
identifying dominant directions of variability







Important


For dynamic systems, PCA by itself does not describe how the system evolves over time. It only summarizes the data in feature space; any temporal information must be introduced explicitly, for example through window-based features or lagged variables, that is, past values of the same signal such as \(x_{t-1}\), \(x_{t-2}\), and so on, used together with the current value \(x_t\) to capture the temporal dynamics

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
SVM: supervised binary classification


Assume labeled feature vectors, where \(\mathbf{x_i}\) is the vector of observations and \(y_i\) is the class label: \[
(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i),\quad y_i \in \{-1,+1\},\quad i=1,\dots,N
\]
The goal is to learn a decision function \[
f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathrm{sign}(g(\mathbf{x}))
\] that separates the two classes with maximum robustness




SVM example in 2-D




For the linear case, \(g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b\) and the separating boundary is the hyperplane \(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0\)

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
SVM: maximum-margin principle
If the classes are linearly separable, the hard-margin SVM solves: \[
\min_{\mathbf{w},b}\frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2
\] subject to \[
y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i+b)\ge1,\quad i=1,\dots,N
\]
The geometric margin is \[
\gamma = \frac{2}{\|\mathbf{w}\|_2}
\]
Therefore, minimizing \(\|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2\) is equivalent to maximizing the separation margin

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
SVM: Interactive Example




We select as decision boundary \(\mathbf{w} = [\cos(\theta), \sin(\theta)]^T\)
\(||\mathbf w||_2 = 1\) (it is a unit vector)
\(b\) the intercept with the vertical axis

The margin width is then \(2/\|\mathbf{w}\|_2 = 2\) and the support vectors are those that satisfy \(y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i+b) = 1\)
Try and find \(\theta, b\) that maximize the margin while correctly classifying all points







Caution


With the hard margin formulation, there is no solution if the data are not linearly separable, which is often the case in real applications. This motivates the introduction of the soft margin formulation, which allows for some misclassifications while still maximizing the margin.

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
SVM: Interactive Example (R version)


We do a linear SVM classification and add predictions to it:

fit <- svm(
  state ~ x1 + x2, data = df, 
  scale = FALSE, kernel = "linear"
)

df <-  df %>% mutate(
  id = 1:n(),
  pred = predict(fit, .),
  sv = if_else(id %in% fit$index, "SV", "Other")
)
coef(fit)

(Intercept)          x1          x2 
       -0.2        -0.8         0.4 


Coefficients are \(b\), \(w_1\), and \(w_2\) in the decision function \(g(\mathbf{x}) = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b\)

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
section: SVM: soft margin and hinge loss
text:
SVM: soft margin and hinge loss



Real process data are noisy, overlapping, and rarely perfectly separable
The soft-margin formulation introduces slack variables \(\xi_i \ge 0\): \[
\min_{\mathbf{w},b,\boldsymbol{\xi}}
\frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{N}\xi_i
\] subject to: \[
y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i+b)\ge1-\xi_i
\]





Soft-margin SVM




The parameter \(C\) balances margin width against classification violations






Note


Equivalent empirical-risk view: \(\min_{\mathbf{w},b}\frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{N}\max(0,1-y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i+b))\)

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
Equivalent empirical-risk view


The same soft-margin SVM can be written as \[
\min_{\mathbf{w},b}\frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2 +
C\sum_{i=1}^{N}\max(0,1-y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i+b))
\]
This has the standard machine-learning form \[
\text{regularization} + \text{empirical loss}
\]

\(\frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2\) penalizes complex models and favors a wider margin
\(\max(0,1-y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i+b))\) is the hinge loss
\(C\) controls the trade-off between margin size and training violations


Interpretation:

if \(y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i+b)\ge1\), the point is correctly classified and outside the margin, so the loss is 0
if \(0<y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i+b)<1\), the point is correctly classified but inside the margin
if \(y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i+b)\le0\), the point is misclassified

So this view is equivalent to the slack-variable formulation:

geometric view: maximize margin while allowing violations
empirical-risk view: minimize regularized hinge loss on the training set

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
section: SVM: dual problem and kernel trick
text:
SVM: dual problem and kernel trick


The dual formulation is \[
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}
\sum_{i=1}^{N}\alpha_i -
\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N}
\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)
\] with constraints \[
\alpha_i \ge 0,\qquad
\sum_{i=1}^{N}\alpha_i y_i = 0
\]

The kernel function \[
K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)=\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T\phi(\mathbf{x}_j)
\] allows nonlinear separation in the original space through linear separation in a transformed feature space
Common kernels:

linear: \(K(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})=\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{z}\)
polynomial
radial basis function, RBF: \[
K(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})=\exp(-\gamma\|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{z}\|_2^2)
\]

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
SVM: understanding the dual problem



Instead of optimizing the hyperplane parameters directly, the dual problem optimizes one coefficient \(\alpha_i\) for each training sample: \[
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}
\sum_{i=1}^{N}\alpha_i -
\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N}
\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^T\mathbf{x}_j
\]
For the soft-margin SVM, the constraints are \[
0 \le \alpha_i \le C,\qquad
\sum_{i=1}^{N}\alpha_i y_i = 0
\]
The primal and dual formulations are equivalent: they lead to the same classifier.

Interpretation:

each \(\alpha_i\) measures how much training point \(i\) contributes to the solution
most \(\alpha_i\) are zero
only points with \(\alpha_i > 0\) matter: these are the support vectors
the upper bound \(C\) limits the influence of a single sample

Why it is useful:

the data appear only through inner products \(\mathbf{x}_i^T\mathbf{x}_j\)
this makes the solution easier to reinterpret in geometric terms
it prepares the ground for replacing inner products with kernels

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
SVM: the kernel trick



In the dual problem and in the decision function, the samples appear only through inner products: \[
\mathbf{x}_i^T\mathbf{x}_j
\]
If we first map the data into a feature space \(\phi(\mathbf{x})\), the same algorithm would use \[
\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T\phi(\mathbf{x}_j)
\]
The kernel trick replaces this inner product with a kernel function \[
K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)=\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T\phi(\mathbf{x}_j)
\] without computing \(\phi(\mathbf{x})\) explicitly.

Why this matters:

a linear separator in feature space becomes a nonlinear boundary in the original space
the optimization problem stays almost unchanged
prediction still depends only on support vectors

Common kernels:

linear: \(K(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})=\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{z}\)
polynomial
radial basis function: \[
K(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{z})=\exp(-\gamma\|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{z}\|_2^2)
\]










Note


Note: the dual formulation is what makes kernelized SVMs possible.

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
section: SVM: decision function and time-series use
text:
SVM: decision function and time-series use
After training, the decision function is \[
g(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i \in SV}\alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}) + b
\] where only the support vectors contribute
For time-series analysis, SVM is applied to features extracted from samples or windows, for example:

direct multivariate measurements \((x_{1,t},x_{2,t},\dots)\)
lagged vectors \((x_t,x_{t-1},\dots,x_{t-L})\)
statistical and spectral features over a window

SVM is therefore a classification rule in feature space, not a stochastic time model

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
Synthetic bivariate time series


We construct a bivariate time series with two process variables:

a normal regime with strong positive correlation
a fault regime with mean shift and changed covariance structure

This is enough to illustrate how PCA detects changes in latent structure

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
PCA Analysis 1/4


We apply PCA to the standardized two-variable feature matrix
Since \(p=2\), the model is simple enough to visualize directly:

loadings describe the principal directions
scores show how observations evolve in the latent space
explained variance quantifies dimensional compression

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
PCA Analysis 2/4
First we do the PCA fit and compute scores \([\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2]\):

pca_fit <- prcomp(~x1+x2, data=ts_df, center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)
summary(pca_fit)

Importance of components:
                          PC1    PC2
Standard deviation     1.3272 0.4884
Proportion of Variance 0.8808 0.1192
Cumulative Proportion  0.8808 1.0000

round(pca_fit$rotation, 3)

      PC1    PC2
x1 -0.707 -0.707
x2 -0.707  0.707

scores <- as_tibble(pca_fit$x) %>% mutate(t = ts_df$t, state = ts_df$state)

We also calculate \(\mathbf \lambda\) and rescale the data:

(lambda <- pca_fit$sdev^2)

[1] 1.761512 0.238488

center_vec <- pca_fit$center
scale_vec <- pca_fit$scale
x_scaled <- scale(ts_df %>% select(x1, x2), center = center_vec, scale = scale_vec)

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
PCA Analysis 3/4
We calculate the residuals vector \(\mathbf\varepsilon = \mathbf{x}_c - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_c\) and the monitoring statistics. Given the \(\lambda\) values, we only keep \(\mathbf{p}_1\) so:
\[
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{c,t}^T = \mathbf{t}_t^T \mathbf{P}_a^T
\]
becomes:

x_hat <- tcrossprod(pca_fit$x[, 1, drop = FALSE], pca_fit$rotation[, 1, drop = FALSE])
residuals_mat <- x_scaled - x_hat

Finally we can calculate the monitoring statistics:

spe <- rowSums(residuals_mat^2)
t2 <- rowSums((pca_fit$x[, 1:2, drop = FALSE]^2) / matrix(rep(lambda[1:2], each = n), ncol = 2))

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
PCA Analysis 4/4


Finally we make an overall plot:

p <- autoplot(
  pca_fit, data = ts_df, 
  colour = "state",
  loadings = TRUE,
  loadings.label = TRUE,
  loadings.label.size = 3
  ) +
  labs(title = "PCA score plot with loadings") +
  coord_fixed() +
  coord_flip()







Note


This is called a biplot. Another useful visualization is the screeplot, which shows the explained variance ratio \(\eta_a\) as a function of the number of retained components \(a\).

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
PCA monitoring statistics


For each time instant we compute:

score coordinates PC1 and PC2
SPE, squared prediction error
Hotelling’s \(T^2\)

For \(T^2\) we can define a control limit associated with a false-alarm probability \(\alpha\): \[
T^2_{\alpha} =
\frac{a(n-1)}{n-a}F_{a,n-a;1-\alpha}
\] where \(a\) is the number of retained components and \(n\) is the reference sample size













In this synthetic example, PCA is mainly used as an exploratory and monitoring tool: it reveals the regime change without using class labels during training.

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
section: SVM on the same bivariate time series
text:
SVM on the same bivariate time series



Now we use the same observations, but with labels Normal and Fault
The feature vector is simply \[
\mathbf{x}_t = [x_{1,t},x_{2,t}]^T
\]
An RBF-kernel SVM is trained to separate the two states:


svm(state ~ x1 + x2, data = ts_df,
    kernel = "radial", cost = 5, gamma = 1,
    scale = TRUE)

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
SVM predicted labels over time


The decision boundary is learned in feature space, but the output can be mapped back to time
This makes it possible to compare:

true process state
SVM predicted state
classification errors and transition regions







Note


This scheme can be carried out continuously in an online setting:

where the SVM is trained on a reference dataset and then applied to new observations as they arrive, providing real-time classification of the process state
or by chunking the time series into windows and applying the SVM to each window

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
Python example: same synthetic dataset
The same bivariate time series can be generated in Python with numpy and stored in a pandas data frame
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

np.random.seed(0)

n = 240
t = np.arange(1, n + 1)
fault_idx = np.arange(150, 210)

x1 = np.cumsum(np.random.normal(0, 0.06, n)) \
     + 0.7 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 24)
x2 = 0.8 * x1 + np.random.normal(0, 0.18, n)

x1[fault_idx] = x1[fault_idx] + 1.0
x2[fault_idx] = -0.2 * x1[fault_idx] \
                + np.random.normal(0, 0.22, len(fault_idx)) + 0.7

state = np.where(np.isin(t, fault_idx + 1), "Fault", "Normal")

df = pd.DataFrame({"t": t, "x1": x1, "x2": x2, "state": state})

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
section: PCA on the bivariate series in Python
text:
PCA on the bivariate series in Python


The PCA workflow in Python is analogous to the R one:

standardize the variables
fit a PCA model
inspect explained variance and loadings
project the observations to obtain principal scores


from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

X = df[["x1", "x2"]].to_numpy()

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

pca = PCA(n_components=2)
scores = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

df["PC1"] = scores[:, 0]
df["PC2"] = scores[:, 1]

print(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)
print(pca.components_)

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
PCA monitoring statistics in Python


As in R, we can reconstruct the retained subspace and compute:

the score trajectories
Hotelling’s \(T^2\)
the residual SPE statistic

This provides a direct parallel with the PCA monitoring slide shown earlier

import numpy as np

lam = pca.explained_variance_
X_hat = scores @ pca.components_
E = X_scaled - X_hat

df["SPE"] = np.sum(E**2, axis=1)
df["T2"] = np.sum((scores**2) / lam, axis=1)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(8, 5), sharex=True)
ax[0].plot(df["t"], df["PC1"])
ax[0].set_ylabel("PC1")
ax[1].plot(df["t"], df["SPE"])
ax[1].set_ylabel("SPE")
ax[1].set_xlabel("Time index")

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
section: SVM on the same bivariate time series in Python
text:
SVM on the same bivariate time series in Python


Using scikit-learn, the same classification problem is solved by fitting an RBF-kernel SVM to the two features
The workflow is:

build X and class labels y
fit SVC
predict the class of each observation
evaluate accuracy and inspect the decision regions


from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

X = df[["x1", "x2"]].to_numpy()
y = df["state"].to_numpy()

svc = SVC(kernel="rbf", C=5.0, gamma=1.0)
svc.fit(X, y)

df["pred"] = svc.predict(X)
acc = accuracy_score(y, df["pred"])
print(acc)

section: SVM decision regions and predicted labels in Python
text:
SVM decision regions and predicted labels in Python


The decision map in feature space can be visualized by evaluating the classifier on a regular grid
The predicted labels can then be plotted back against time exactly as done in the R example

x1g = np.linspace(df["x1"].min() - 0.3, df["x1"].max() + 0.3, 160)
x2g = np.linspace(df["x2"].min() - 0.3, df["x2"].max() + 0.3, 160)
xx1, xx2 = np.meshgrid(x1g, x2g)
Xg = np.column_stack([xx1.ravel(), xx2.ravel()])
zg = svc.predict(Xg).reshape(xx1.shape)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))
ax[0].contourf(xx1, xx2, (zg == "Fault").astype(float), alpha=0.25)
ax[0].scatter(df["x1"], df["x2"], c=(df["state"] == "Fault"))
ax[0].set_xlabel("x1")
ax[0].set_ylabel("x2")

ax[1].plot(df["t"], df["state"].eq("Fault").astype(int), label="True")
ax[1].plot(df["t"], df["pred"].eq("Fault").astype(int), "--",
           label="Predicted")
ax[1].set_xlabel("Time index")
ax[1].legend()

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
PCA and SVM: complementary roles
PCA and SVM serve different purposes:

PCA is unsupervised and looks for directions of maximum variance
SVM is supervised and looks for a boundary that best separates known classes

In practice they are often combined:

PCA for denoising or dimensionality reduction
SVM for classification on the retained principal scores

For high-dimensional time-series features this PCA plus SVM pipeline is often more stable than direct classification on raw variables alone

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
Hotelling’s \(T^2\) distribution
Hotelling’s \(T^2\) is the multivariate extension of Student’s \(t\) test when the parameter of interest is a mean vector rather than a scalar mean
If \[
\mathbf{x}_1,\dots,\mathbf{x}_n \in \mathbb{R}^p
\] are independent observations from a multivariate normal population, the one-sample null hypothesis is \[
H_0:\boldsymbol{\mu}=\boldsymbol{\mu}_0
\]
The sample mean vector and covariance matrix are \[
\bar{\mathbf{x}}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbf{x}_i,
\qquad
\mathbf{S}=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}
(\mathbf{x}_i-\bar{\mathbf{x}})
(\mathbf{x}_i-\bar{\mathbf{x}})^T
\]

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
One-sample Hotelling’s \(T^2\)
The test statistic is \[
T^2 = n(\bar{\mathbf{x}}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_0)^T
\mathbf{S}^{-1}
(\bar{\mathbf{x}}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_0)
\]
Interpretation:

\((\bar{\mathbf{x}}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_0)\) is the deviation from the target mean vector
\(\mathbf{S}^{-1}\) scales that deviation by the covariance structure
large values of \(T^2\) indicate that the observed mean is too far from the target to be explained by normal variability

For \(p=1\), this reduces to the usual Student’s \(t^2\)

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
Relation with the F distribution
Under multivariate normality and under \(H_0\), the statistic can be converted into an F test: \[
F = \frac{n-p}{p(n-1)}T^2
\] with \[
F \sim F_{p,n-p}
\]
Therefore, at significance level \(\alpha\), we reject \(H_0\) when \[
T^2 >
\frac{p(n-1)}{n-p}F_{p,n-p;1-\alpha}
\]
This gives an exact critical region for the one-sample test

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
Two-sample Hotelling’s \(T^2\)
If two independent populations are compared, \[
H_0:\boldsymbol{\mu}_1=\boldsymbol{\mu}_2
\] the pooled-covariance version of the statistic is \[
T^2 =
\frac{n_1 n_2}{n_1+n_2}
(\bar{\mathbf{x}}_1-\bar{\mathbf{x}}_2)^T
\mathbf{S}_p^{-1}
(\bar{\mathbf{x}}_1-\bar{\mathbf{x}}_2)
\] where \[
\mathbf{S}_p =
\frac{(n_1-1)\mathbf{S}_1 + (n_2-1)\mathbf{S}_2}
{n_1+n_2-2}
\]
This is the multivariate analogue of the classical two-sample t test

title: Feature-based analysis of time series
text:
Assumptions and interpretation
Hotelling’s \(T^2\) is appropriate when:

observations are independent
the population is approximately multivariate normal
the covariance matrix is nonsingular

Its main advantage over multiple univariate t tests is that it tests the mean vector jointly, accounting for correlation between variables
This avoids contradictory conclusions caused by ignoring covariance structure

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)
The FFT is an efficient algorithm to compute the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of a sequence, which transforms a time-domain signal into its frequency-domain representation. The DFT is defined as: \[X_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_n e^{-2\pi i kn/N}\] where:

\(X_k \in \mathbb{C}\) is the DFT of the sequence at frequency bin \(k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1\)
\(x_n \in \mathbb{C}\) is the input sequence at time index \(n\)
\(N\) is the total number of samples in the input sequence

The FFT reduces the computational complexity from \(O(N^2)\) to \(O(N \log N)\), making it feasible to analyze large datasets efficiently

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
Power Spectral Density (PSD)
The Power Spectral Density (PSD) is a measure of the power distribution of a signal across different frequency components. It is defined as the squared magnitude of the Fourier Transform of the signal, normalized by the length of the signal: \[PSD(f) = \frac{1}{N} |X_k|^2\] where:

\(PSD(f)\) is the power spectral density at frequency \(f\)
\(N\) is the total number of samples in the signal
\(X_k\) is the Fourier Transform of the signal at frequency bin \(k\)

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
FFT: to remember
Input vector is normally a sequence of real numbers, but it can also be complex. The output is a sequence of complex numbers, where the magnitude represents the amplitude of the corresponding frequency component, and the phase represents its phase shift
If the input sequence is real-valued, the output will be symmetric around the Nyquist frequency, which is half the sampling rate. The FFT can be used to analyze the frequency content of signals, identify dominant frequencies, and perform various signal processing tasks such as filtering and spectral analysis
If the input sequence is complex-valued, the output will not be symmetric, and it can represent a wider range of frequency components, including negative frequencies. The FFT is widely used in various applications, including audio processing, image analysis, and communications
Frequency resolution of the FFT is determined by the sampling rate and the length of the input sequence. The frequency bins are spaced at intervals of \(\frac{f_s}{N}\), where \(f_s\) is the sampling rate and \(N\) is the number of samples in the input sequence

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text: FFT: to remember (Effect of \(N\), 1/2)

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text: FFT: to remember (Effect of \(N\), 2/2)

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
FFT: Windowing and Spectral Leakage
When performing an FFT on a finite-length signal, the assumption is that the signal is periodic. If the signal does not complete an integer number of periods within the window, it can lead to spectral leakage, where energy from one frequency bin leaks into adjacent bins, distorting the frequency representation. To mitigate this issue, windowing functions are applied to the signal before performing the FFT.
Common windowing functions include:

Hann: \(w(n) = 0.5 \left(1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)\right)\)
Hamming: \(w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)\)
Blackman: \(w(n) = 0.42 - 0.5 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) + 0.08 \cos\left(\frac{4\pi n}{N-1}\right)\)

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text: Example: Windowing and Spectral Leakage

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
Frequency-domain analysis of data streams
In real-time applications, data streams are often analyzed using a technique called Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT), which involves applying the FFT to short, overlapping segments of the data stream. This allows for tracking how the frequency content of the signal evolves over time. The STFT is defined as: \[STFT\{x(t)\}(m, \omega) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) w(n - m) e^{-j \omega n}\] where:

\(x(n)\) is the input signal
\(w(n - m)\) is a window function centered at time index \(m\)
\(\omega\) is the angular frequency

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
section: Example: STFT of a data stream
text:
Example: STFT of a data stream






Note


The STFT provides a time-frequency representation of the signal, allowing for the analysis of non-stationary signals whose frequency content changes over time. This is particularly useful in applications such as speech processing, music analysis, and machine condition monitoring, where the characteristics of the signal can vary significantly over time.

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
Monitoring a signal in real-time

FFT and STFT work in batch mode, meaning they require a block of data to perform the analysis. They are a powerful tool for screening the frequency content of signals, but they are not immediately designed for real-time monitoring of data streams
They can be applied to overlapping windows of the data stream to provide a continuous analysis, but there is a trade-off between time resolution and frequency resolution. The choice of window size and overlap will depend on the characteristics of the signal being analyzed and the computational resources available
For real-time monitoring, techniques such as Goertzel algorithm or recursive DFT can be used, which allow for continuous analysis of the signal as new data arrives without needing to wait for a full block of data.

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
Recursive DFT


The Recursive DFT is an efficient algorithm for computing the DFT of a signal in real-time. It allows for the continuous analysis of a signal as new data arrives without needing to wait for a full block of data. The recursive DFT is defined as: \[X_k(n) = X_k(n-1) + x(n) - x(n-N)\] where:

\(X_k(n)\) is the DFT coefficient at time \(n\) for frequency bin \(k\)
\(x(n)\) is the input signal at time \(n\)
\(N\) is the length of the DFT








Fast implementation of the Recursive DFT: SlidingDFT


This can be used in C, C++ and Python:

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
Goertzel algorithm


The Goertzel algorithm is a computationally efficient method for calculating specific DFT coefficients, particularly useful for applications like tone detection in telephony. It is defined as: \[s(n) = x(n) + 2 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) s(n-1) - s(n-2)\]

\(x(n)\) is the input signal at time \(n\)
\(s(n)\) is the state variable at time \(n\)
\(k\) is the index of the frequency bin being analyzed
\(N\) is the length of the DFT








Goertzel library


This can be used in C, C++, Python, and R:   











Tip


The Goertzel algorithm is particularly efficient for calculating a small number of DFT coefficients, as it avoids the need to compute the entire DFT. It is commonly used in applications such as DTMF tone detection, where only specific frequencies need to be analyzed.

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
Goertzel algorithm: example






















bins <- frequencies * N / fs
response <- goertzel::goertzel(df$signal, bins)
power <- Mod(response) ^ 2

low_index <- which.max(power[seq_along(dtmf_low)])
high_index <- which.max(power[length(dtmf_low) + 
  seq_along(dtmf_high)])

key <- matrix(
  c("1", "2", "3", "A",
    "4", "5", "6", "B",
    "7", "8", "9", "C",
    "*", "0", "#", "D"),
  nrow = length(dtmf_low),
  byrow = TRUE
)
key[low_index, high_index]

[1] "6"

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
The timescale problem
When continuously monitoring a process, we have to deal with multivariate timeseries data that can be sampled at different rates. For example, in a machining process, we might have:

Vibration data sampled at a high rate (e.g., 10 kHz) to capture high-frequency components related to tool wear or chatter
Power data sampled at a lower rate (e.g., 100 Hz) to track the cutting forces during machining
Controller status updated at variable intervals to monitor the state of the machine







Tip


Any continuous, feature-based technique for process monitoring can’t reasonably run at the maximum frequency of the data, so we need to find a way to extract meaningful features from the data at different timescales and integrate them into a unified monitoring system.

title: Frequency-domain analysis of time series
text:
Feature extraction and integration
To integrate frequency-domain analysis into a real-time monitoring system, we can follow these steps for Feature Extraction
We use FFT or SFFT to extract power in specific bands of interest:

in chunks of data: if computation time exceeds sampling time, there’s a risk of falling behind and loosing information
with sliding window approach to continuously update the features as new data arrives, only updating the extracted features at a lower rate than the raw data sampling rate
in overlapping chunks of data avoid the risk of loosing information between updates, but it can increase computational load

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Time-series statistics
All regression methods seen so far are based on the assumption that the random variable is \(x\overset{IID}\sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\). That is, all observations must be uncorrelated
This assumption is, among other things, the basis for the recommendation to randomize the operating sequence
Suppose we can treat a measurement as a time-dependent signal. It is clear that by reducing the signal sampling interval, sooner or later each sample will be correlated with the previous one
There is therefore a maximum sampling frequency above which every measurement turns out to be autocorrelated, that is, the observations of \(x\) are no longer IID
This situation occurs when the dynamics of the measuring instrument or of the measurand itself, which are always finite, are slower than the time interval at which measurements are taken

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Time-series statistics


Consider, for example, the time series in the figure, which reports the difference between the mean temperature of land areas and the corresponding mean value over the period 1951–1980
It is clear that nearby observations are more correlated than distant ones
Moreover, it is clear, and relevant, to evaluate the dependence of the random variable on time in order to make future predictions

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Autocovariance and autocorrelation
We have seen how the covariance and correlation operators are used to estimate the independence of two samples
Considering a time-dependent signal \(x=x(t)\), it is interesting to consider the covariance of the signal with itself, shifted in time
We define the autocovariance function \(\gamma(s,t)\) as the function that evaluates the covariance of a time signal with itself evaluated starting at times \(s\) and \(t\): \[
\gamma_x(s, t) = \sigma_{x_s, x_t} = E[(x_s-\mu_s)(x_t-\mu_t)]
\]
It is clear that \(\gamma_x(s,s)=\sigma^2(x_s)\)
The autocorrelation function (ACF), consequently, is defined as: \[
\rho_x(s, t) = \frac{\gamma_x(s,t)}{\sqrt{\gamma_x(s,s)\gamma_x(t,t)}}
\] and has the advantage of always lying in \([-1,1]\). It is also clear that \(\rho_x(s,s)=1\)

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Autocorrelation
If we sample a continuous signal at fixed intervals \(\Delta t\) over a total duration \(T\), we obtain a finite time series of \(N=T/\Delta t\) observations: \(x_{0}=\left<x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\right>\)
We can extend the definition by setting \(s=t_0 = 0\) as the initial instant of the series and \(t=s+\tau\) as a generic later instant such that \[
\tau = \ell \Delta t
\] where \(\ell\) is the delay or lag, and then the autocovariance and autocorrelation for a finite time series become: \[
\gamma_x(\ell) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N-\ell} (x_i - \bar x_0) (x_{i+\ell}-\bar x_\ell) }{N-\ell-2},\quad \rho_x(\ell) = \frac{\gamma_x(\ell)}{\sqrt{\sigma_x(0)\sigma_x(\ell)}}
\] where \[
\bar x_0=\frac{1}{N-\ell}\sum\nolimits_{i=1}^{N-\ell} x_i,\quad \bar x_\ell=\frac{1}{N-\ell}\sum\nolimits_{i=\ell}^{N} x_i
\]

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Autocorrelogram (ACF)












To construct the ACF plot of a time series, the axis is shifted by \(\ell\) samples, neglecting the first \(\ell\) samples in the unshifted series and the last \(\ell\) samples in the shifted one
Then the autocorrelation between the two series is computed
The process is repeated for \(\ell=0, 1, \dots, n\), with \(n\) chosen as a function of the series length, typically at least 50 and in any case no more than half the length of the time series

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Autocorrelogram (ACF)


The autocorrelation function \(\rho_x(\ell)\), which is a discrete-valued function, can be plotted to study the maximum lag beyond which the historical series \(x\) is no longer autocorrelated
The figure shows the signal of a microphone recording the sound “AAHH”. The series is clearly periodic every 0.01 s
The autocorrelogram shows high autocorrelation up to \(\ell=4\). Then the pattern is periodic, confirming that the time series is autocorrelated with itself about every 10 lags


Time seriesACF




























ACF stands for Auto-Correlation Function; the blue highlighted band is the 95% confidence interval. Also, in general, \(\rho_x(0)=1\) always holds

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Autocorrelogram (ACF)


Consider the same data as the previous time series, but sampled at random instants
Then \(y\overset{IID}\sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\) and therefore \(\rho_y(\ell) = 0~\forall \ell>0\)
As expected, the only ACF value outside the confidence interval is \(\rho_x(0)\)
In this case one also says that the time series is a random walk


Time seriesACF

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Stationary time series
A time series may or may not be stationary. The following are defined:

Time series stationary in the wide sense

It is a time series for which the probabilistic behavior of any collection of values \(\left<x_{t_1}, x_{t_2},\dots, x_{t_k}\right>\) is identical to that of the shifted collection \(\left<x_{t_1+h}, x_{t_2+h},\dots, x_{t_k+h}\right>\), that is: \[
\mathrm{Pr}(x_{t_1}\leq c_1, \dots, x_{t_k}\leq c_k) = \mathrm{Pr}(x_{t_1+h}\leq c_1, \dots, x_{t_k+h}\leq c_k).
\]

Time series stationary in the strict sense

It is a time series for which the mean value of the series is constant, independent of time, and the autocovariance function \(\gamma(s,t)\) depends on \(s\) and \(t\) only through their difference \(|s-t|\)


For a wide-sense stationary series one may assume \(\sigma_x(0)=\sigma_x(\ell)=\sigma_x\) and \(\bar x_0=\bar x_\ell\) and, therefore, \(\rho_x(\ell) = \gamma_x(\ell)/\sigma_x\)

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Stabilization

Time series stationary at least in the wide sense are easier to handle
It is therefore useful to try to stabilize the time series by separating it into a trend term \(x_t\) plus a stationary term \(x_s\)
stabilization can be done in two ways:

detrending by linear regression: \(x_t = x_{l,t} + x_{s,t}\), where \(x_{l,t} = a + bt\) and, therefore, \(x_{s,t}\) is the residual series of the linear regression of \(x_t\)
detrending by differencing

The stabilized time series can then be analyzed and then transformed back by the inverse operation:

adding the trend term
integration, that is cumulative sum

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text: Stabilization (example)

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Differencing operators
In general, stabilization by differencing gives better results and is also more practical: if the differenced time series is not stable, the differencing order can be increased until stability is reached
How is a time series differenced?
The following are defined:

Backshift operator: this is the operator \(B^n\) such that \(B^n x_t := x_{t-n}\)
Difference operator: this is the operator \(\nabla\) such that \(\nabla x_t := x_t - x_{t-1}=(1-B)x_t\). It follows that \(\nabla^dx_t=(1-B)^dx_t\), and therefore for example \(\nabla^2x_t=(1-B)^2x_t=x_t-2x_{t-1}+x_{t-2}\)

So, for example, differencing \(\nabla^2x_t\) is the discrete equivalent of the second derivative \(\frac{d^2}{dt^2}x(t)\) for the continuous function \(x(t)\)

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text: Stabilization and ACF

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Stabilization and ACF



The ACF of a non-stabilized series always shows correlation even at high lags
The ACF of the residual series from a linear regression decreases faster, but still keeps correlation even at high lags
The ACF of the differenced series, moreover, damps out very quickly, the lags 1, 2, 3, … should be exponential, after which it shows harmonic oscillations, indicating a periodicity in the original time series














We can therefore say that the historical chicken price series shows a trend that stabilizes at first-order differencing, has dynamics that show autocorrelation up to the previous three points, and a periodic pattern with period equal to 12 lags.

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
AR models — definition
An Auto-Regressive (AR) model expresses a given observation \(x_t\) at time \(t\) as a linear combination of \(p\) previous values \(x_{t-1}, x_{t-2},\dots,x_{t-p}\). An AR model of order \(p\), abbreviated as \(\mathrm{AR}(p)\), has the form: \[
x_t=\phi_1 x_{t-1} + \phi_2 x_{t-2}+\dots+\phi_p x_{t-p} + w_t \label{eq:AR}
\]

\(x_t\) is wide-sense stationary and \(w_t\sim\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_w)\)
\(\phi_1, \phi_2,\dots,\phi_p\) are constants and \(\phi_p\neq0\)

If the mean of \(x_t\) is not zero, \(x_t\) is replaced with \(x_t - \mu\) to obtain: \[
\begin{align}
x_t-\mu&=\phi_1( x_{t-1}-\mu) + \phi_2 (x_{t-2}-\mu)+\dots+\phi_p (x_{t-p}-\mu) + w_t \\
x_t&=\alpha + \phi_1 x_{t-1} + \phi_2 x_{t-2}+\dots+\phi_p x_{t-p} + w_t,~~~\alpha=\mu(1-\phi_1-\phi_2-\dots-\phi_p)
\end{align}
\]


The \(w_t\) are also called innovations, since they are the only new contribution of a point with respect to the previous ones.

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
AR models — regression
Recalling the definition of the backshift operator, the definition of \(w_t\) can be written as:
\[
(1-\phi_1 B - \phi_2 B^2 -\dots-\phi_p B^p)x_t=w_t
\] o ancora più concisamente come:
\[
\Phi_p(B)x_t=w_t
\] where \(\Phi_p(B):=1-\phi_1B-\phi_2B^2-\dots-\phi_pB^p\) is called the autoregressive operator.
Performing the regression of an \(\mathrm{AR}(p)\) model on a historical series \(x_t\) therefore means fitting the model \(\Phi_p(B)\hat{ x_t}=w_t\) by identifying the coefficients of \(\Phi_p(B)\) that minimize the mean squared residuals, where the residuals are \(\varepsilon_t = x_t - \hat{ x_t} = x_t - \Phi_p^{-1}(B)w_t\), assuming the inverse \(\Phi_p^{-1}(B)\) exists.

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
MA models — definition
Alternatively, one can imagine the case in which the generic observation \(x_t\) is expressed as a linear combination of the disturbance acting on the previous \(q\) observations. An MA model of order \(q\), abbreviated as \(\mathrm{MA}(q)\), is defined as \[
x_t = w_t + \theta_1 w_{t-1} + \theta_2 w_{t-2} + \dots + \theta_q w_{t-q}
\]

\(w_t\sim\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)\)
\(\theta_1, \theta_2,\dots,\theta_q\) are constant parameters with \(\theta_q\neq0\)

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
MA models — regression
Similarly to the \(\mathrm{AR}(p)\) case, for \(\mathrm{MA}(q)\) it is possible to define the moving-average operator of order \(q\): \[
\Theta_q(B)=(1+\theta_1 B+\theta_2 B^2+\dots+\theta_q B^q)
\] such that the equation for \(x_t\) can be written as:
\[
x_t=\Theta_q(B)w_t
\]
As above, regressing an \(\mathrm{MA}(q)\) model onto a historical series \(x_t\) means identifying the terms of \(\Theta_q(B)\) that minimize the mean squared residuals, defined as \(\varepsilon_t = x_t - \hat{x_t} = x_t - \Theta_q(B)\), note that this time there is no inverse.

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
MA models and ACF


An \(MA(q)\) time series has a memory extending up to lag \(q\), in the sense that innovations at distances greater than \(q\) have no effect on the last observation
Therefore, given an \(MA(q)\) time series, one can often identify the order from its ACF by counting the peaks after the one at lag 0:

three peaks outside the confidence band mean an \(MA(3)\) model
the signs of the peaks correspond to the signs of the coefficients

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
MA models and ACF


If instead the time series is of type \(AR(p)\), each observation depends on the innovation and on all previous observations, recursively
In this case the autocorrelogram will show an exponential decay possibly followed by harmonic oscillations

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
AR models and PACF
For models of type \(AR(p)\) it is still possible to identify the order through the partial autocorrelation function, or PACF, defined as follows:
\[
\begin{align}
\mathrm{PACF}_1 &= \mathrm{ACF}(z_{t+1}, z_t) \\
\mathrm{PACF}_k &= \mathrm{ACF}(z_{t+k} - \hat{z_{t+k}}, z_t - \hat{z_t}),~~~k\geq2 \\
\end{align}
\] where \(\hat{z_{t+k}}\) and \(\hat{z_t}\) are linear combinations of \(\{z_{t+1}, z_{t+2},\dots,z_{t+k-1}\}\) that minimize the mean squared error of \(z_{t+k}\) and \(z_t\), respectively

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
AR models and PACF


In general, therefore, if the ACF shows an infinite memory, AR model, and the PACF shows few peaks, the number of peaks is the order of the AR model
Warning: peaks after the first cut-off are not considered, that is, the lag at which the autocorrelation falls below the confidence limit for the first time













Warning: in these ACF and PACF plots the lag starts from 1.

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
MA models and PACF


Comparing both ACF and PACF

pure noise: ACF and PACF are zero for lag greater than 0
\(AR(p)\): ACF decreases slowly, PACF is non-zero for lags less than or equal to \(p\), zero otherwise
\(MA(q)\): ACF shows \(q\) peaks; if \(PACF(1) > 0\), PACF oscillates to 0, otherwise it decays geometrically to 0














Often, in practice, these indications are hard to observe and it is therefore difficult to identify \(p,q\) in a certain and unambiguous way

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
ARMA models
The obvious extension comes from combining the \(\mathrm{AR}(p)\) and \(\mathrm{MA}(q)\) models
An ARMA model of order \(p,q\), abbreviated as \(\mathrm{ARMA}(p,q)\), is defined as:
\[
x_t=\phi_1 x_{t-1} + \phi_2 x_{t-2}+\dots+\phi_p x_{t-p} + w_t + \theta_1 w_{t-1} + \theta_2 w_{t-2} + \dots + \theta_q w_{t-q}
\] where \(\phi_p\) and \(\theta_q\) are non-zero, or more briefly: \[
\Phi_p(B)x_t=\Theta_q(B)w_t
\]

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
MA models and PACF


Comparing both ACF and PACF

pure noise: ACF and PACF are zero for lag greater than 0
\(AR(p)\): ACF decreases slowly, PACF is non-zero for lags less than or equal to \(p\), zero otherwise
\(MA(q)\): ACF shows \(q\) peaks; if \(PACF(1) > 0\), PACF oscillates to 0, otherwise it decays geometrically to 0
\(ARMA(p,q)\): ACF shows \(q\) peaks; PACF decays geometrically to 0 only after lag \(p\)














Often, in practice, these indications are hard to observe and it is therefore difficult to identify \(p,q\) in a certain and unambiguous way

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
ARIMA models
The \(\mathrm{ARMA}(p,q)\) processes are suitable only for describing stationary series in the wide sense
We have seen, however, that a non-stationary process can be made stationary by differencing of a suitable degree \(d\)
A process \(x_t\) is called \(\mathrm{ARIMA}(p,d,q)\) when \(\nabla^d x_t = (1-B)^d x_t\) is an \(\mathrm{ARMA}(p,q)\) process
In general, when \(E(\nabla^dx_t)=0\), the \(\mathrm{ARIMA}(p,d,q)\) process can be written as: \[
\Phi_p(B)\nabla^dx_t=\Theta_q(B)w_t
\] If instead the expected value \(E(\nabla^dx_t)=\mu\), then: \[
\Phi_p(B)\nabla^dx_t=\delta + \Theta_q(B)w_t,~~~\delta=\mu(1-\phi_1-\dots-\phi_p)
\]

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Stability and uniqueness of models
For an \(\mathrm{MA}(1)\) model, instead, consider two models: \[
\begin{align}
x_t &= w_t + \theta w_{t-1}, ~w_t\sim\mathcal{N}(0, 1) \\
y_t &= \nu_t + 1/\theta \nu_{t-1}, ~\nu_t\sim\mathcal{N}(0, \theta^2)
\end{align}
\] It is clear that \(x_t\) and \(\nu_t\) have the same ACF and are indistinguishable, since we do not know the innovations \(w_t\) and \(\nu_t\) but only the two series. It can be shown that the result is general, that is, it does not depend on the order \(q\).
It is therefore necessary to choose one of the two alternative forms. To determine which one, we rewrite the series as \(w_t=-\theta x_{t-1}+x_t\), which has the same form as \(\mathrm{AR}(1)\). We can then choose the alternative that satisfies the same stability criterion: \[
\left|\sum_{i=1}^q \theta_i\right| < 1
\] This alternative is called invertible

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Stability and uniqueness of models
In general, it can be shown that the criteria seen above correspond to imposing the requirement that the complex roots of the polynomials \[
\begin{align}
\Phi_p(z)&=1-\phi_1 z-\phi_2 z^2 - \dots -\phi_p z^p \\
\Theta_q(z)&=1+\theta_1 z+\theta_2 z^2 + \dots +\theta_q z^q
\end{align}
\] are all strictly outside the unit circle in the complex plane
For example: \(\mathrm{ARMA}(2,2)\) with \(\Phi_2(z)= 1 + 0.9z + 0.1z^2\) and \(\theta_q(z) = 1 + 2z + 15z^2\):

abs(polyroot(c(1, -0.9, -0.1)))

[1]  1 10

abs(polyroot(c(1, 2, 15)))

[1] 0.2581989 0.2581989


That is, the AR term is not causal and the MA term is not invertible, but \(\theta_q(z) = 1 + 1/2z + 1/15z^2\) would be

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Redundancy
Consider the process \(x_t = w_t\), with \(w_t\sim\mathcal{N}(0, 1)\): this is obviously pure random noise
Multiply both sides by \(1-0.5B\) to obtain: \[
x_t = 0.5x_{t-1}-0.5w_{t-1} + w_t
\] which looks like an \(\mathrm{ARMA}(1,1)\) process but is obviously still the same random noise. How do we distinguish these cases?

factorize the polynomials \(\Phi_p\) and \(\Theta_q\)
remove the common factors
recombine the factors to obtain the non-redundant model

In R one can still use polyroot()

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Redundancy
For example, consider the process \(x_t=0.4x_{t-1}+0.45x_{t-2}+w_t + w_{t-1}+0.25w_{t-2}\), which using the operator \(B\) becomes: \[
(1-0.4B-0.45B^2)x_t = (1+B+0.25B^2)w_t.
\]
In this form the process looks like \(\mathrm{ARMA}(2,2)\), however we can factor the two polynomials using polyroot() to compute the roots:

# per Phi:
-1/polyroot(c(1, -0.4, -0.45))

[1] -0.9-6.887768e-21i  0.5+2.125854e-21i

# per Theta:
-1/polyroot(c(1, 1, 0.25))

[1] 0.5+0i 0.5+0i




Note: if \(z_i,~ i=1,\dots,n\) are the roots of the polynomial \(p(z)\) of degree \(n\), then the polynomial can be factorized into \((1-1/z_1 z)\cdot(1-1/z_2 z)\dots(1-1/z_n z)\).

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Redundancy
As can be seen, one can write \[
\begin{align}
\Phi_p(B)&=(1-0.9B)(1+0.5B) \\
\Theta_q(B)&=(1+0.5B)^2
\end{align}
\]
Removing the common factor \((1+0.5B)\) we obtain the model \[
x_t=0.9x_{t-1}+0.5w_{t-1}+w_t
\] which is an \(\mathrm{ARMA}(1,1)\).
Therefore, in addition to the criteria of causality and invertibility, one must add the criterion of non-redundancy of the parameters, which is checked by removing every common factor from the factorization of the polynomials \(\Phi_p(B)\) and \(\Theta_q(B)\).

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
(S)ARIMA regression
From what has been said above, a process, or time series, \(x_t\) can be regressed by means of an ARIMA model by identifying its parameters through minimization of squared deviations
However, before regression it is necessary to define suitable indices \(p, d,q\), such that there is neither underfitting nor overfitting
In some cases, moreover, series are periodic: besides a possible trend they are also subject to cyclic oscillating patterns. In these cases:

the time series is made stationary by differencing
a low-frequency contribution, called seasonal, is separated from a high-frequency contribution
the seasonal and non-seasonal contributions are regressed separately as two distinct and superposed ARIMA processes: this regression is called Seasonal ARIMA, or SARIMA

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
(S)ARIMA regression
Moreover, we have seen that ARIMA models are based on the assumption of time series stationary in the wide sense
Therefore, it is necessary that both the mean value and the variance are constant over time

The mean is stabilized by differencing
The variance can be stabilized through Box-Cox transformations

Therefore, in the most general case, the model is \(\mathrm{SARIMA}(p, d, q, p_s, d_s, q_s, \lambda)\)
Before performing a regression it is therefore necessary to define the values of the seven parameters, avoiding overfitting and underfitting

\(d, d_s\) can be identified by trial, gradually increasing the values, first \(d\) and then \(d_s\), until the ACF is satisfactory
the other terms can be identified by checking the goodness of the regression over a grid of combinations and evaluating a suitable figure of merit

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Akaike Information Criterion
The most used figure of merit in SARIMA regression is the Akaike Information Criterion, or AIC
In general, the quality of a regression with \(k\) parameters is measured by the sum of squared residuals, \(SS_E(k)\), normalized by the sample size \(n\): \[
\hat{\sigma_k^2} = \frac{SS_E(k)}{n}
\] This indicator is called the Maximum Likelihood Estimator, MLE. The smaller this value is, the better the model fits the data
However, increasing \(k\) decreases the MLE, at the risk of overfitting. For this reason, Akaike proposed penalizing the MLE with the number of parameters: \[
\mathrm{AIC}=\log(\hat{\sigma_k^2})+\frac{n+2k}{n}
\] The AIC must obviously be minimized

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Other information criteria
Besides the AIC there are also the corrected AIC and the Bayesian Information Criterion, or BIC:

Corrected AIC: \(\mathrm{AIC}_c = \log(\hat{\sigma_k^2})+\frac{n+k}{n-k-2}\)
BIC: \(\mathrm{BIC} = \log(\hat{\sigma_k^2})+\frac{k\log(n)}{n}\)

The BIC penalizes model size more strongly, and is therefore preferred for very large samples, thousands of observations, for which AIC and AICc would tend to favor unnecessarily complex models, too many parameters, overfitting
Like the AIC, AICc and BIC must also be minimized

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Regression
The R library forecast provides the function auto.arima(), which evaluates the indicators over a grid of combinations of the seven parameters and returns the best regression:



forecast::auto.arima(AirPassengers, lambda="auto", trace=T)

title: Time-domain analysis of time series
text:
Forecasting


Once the most suitable model has been selected, the one with minimum AIC, one can proceed with regression and prediction
Prediction can be obtained with the forecast() function
This function also reports the 95% confidence bands


ForecastDetail

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Challenges
Acquiring multiple data from a variety of sensors and sources in a manufacturing environment poses several challenges:

Heterogeneous data sources and formats
Real-time processing requirements
Large volumes of data: storage and bandwidth constraints
Scalability and reliability
Network- and Device-neutral architectures

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Solutions

Edge computing for local processing and filtering (bandwidth reduction)
Distributed IIoT data acquisition architectures (e.g., MQTT, ZeroMQ, OPC-UA)
Cloud-based platforms for centralized storage and analysis
Machine learning models for anomaly detection and predictive maintenance

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Where to process data?
Some measurement have high dimensionality (e.g., vibration spectra, 2-D or 3-Dimages) or high sampling rates (e.g., 10 kHz), which can lead to large data volumes
Edge computing allows for local processing and filtering of data, reducing the bandwidth requirements for transmitting raw data over networks
This can involve techniques such as:

feature extraction
dimensionality reduction
anomaly detection

at the edge, allowing only relevant information to be sent to the cloud or central servers for further analysis.

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Edge Computing - Real-time processing
Edge computing also can enable hard real-time processing of data (i.e. time-deterministic processing with guaranteed response times, order of 10–100 microseconds)
Such kind of processing is not trivial on general-purpose computing platforms (because of multithreading and other OS overheads), but can be achieved with:

Real-time operating systems (e.g., FreeRTOS, RTLinux) (☠️☠️☠️)
Field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) (☠️💰💰)
Application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs) or digital signal processors (DSPs) (☠️☠️💰)
bare-metal machine control units (MCUs)







Note


Bare-metal MCus are microcontrollers without OS, which are small, low-power computing devices that can be used for real-time processing of sensor data in edge computing applications. Some of those (Arduino) are very cheap and quick for prototyping, but they have limited processing power and memory. More powerful MCUs (e.g., STM32, ESP32) can handle more complex tasks and larger data volumes, but they may require more development effort and cost.

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
IIoT Protocols


Network communication happens in a layered fashion, according to the OSI model
We are mostly interested in layers 5–7 (host layers):

responsible for application-level communication and data exchange
easily customizable at the user level





OSI network model










Note


Most famous protocols (eg HTTP, FTP, SMTP) are designed for human-in-the-loop applications, are often point-to-point, and are not suitable for machine-to-machine communication, with low latency and high reliability

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
IIoT Protocols requirements

Low latency
Low overhead
Simplified support for complex network topologies (e.g., publish-subscribe, peer-to-peer)
Scalability and reliability
Security features (e.g., encryption, authentication)







Important


IoP communication is focused on Human-to-Machine (H2M) interactions, where the mass of transmitted data is often more important than its frequency (eg a movie or a video stream), and where latency is not critical.
In contrast, IIoT communication is focused on Machine-to-Machine (M2M) interactions, where low latency and high reliability are crucial, and where the volume of transmitted data per communication can be limited (eg a single scalar).

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Network topologies
H2M communication almost always happens in a point-to-point fashion, where a client (e.g., a web browser) requests data from a server (e.g., a web server), and the server responds with the requested data.
In contrast, in M2M communication, more complex network topologies are often required, where multiple devices need to communicate with each other in a more flexible and scalable way. Using point-to-point communication in such scenarios can lead to scalability issues, as the number of connections grows exponentially with the number of devices, and keeping track of network addresses can become unmanageable/error prone






Tip


Common network topologies for M2M communication include:

Publish-subscribe: devices can publish data to a topic, and other devices can subscribe to that topic to receive updates; traffic is managed by a central broker that handles message distribution
Dealer-worker: a device (dealer) can distribute messages to multiple, identical other devices (workers) in a round-robin fashion, with or without a central broker

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Publish-subscribe architectures


Publish-Subscribe architectures are very flexible and suitable for distributed data acquisition
Data producers (publishers) send data to broker, which distributes the data to interested consumers (subscribers) based on subscriptions: no need for direct communication between publishers and subscribers
Only the IP address or hostname of the broker needs to be known by devices: simple network management and possible dynamic addition or removal of devices (scalability)

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Commonly used IIoT protocols
The most commonly used IIoT protocols include:

MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport): a lightweight publish-subscribe protocol designed for low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks; widely used in IIoT applications for its simplicity and efficiency (simple, limited architectures)
ZeroMQ (Zero Message Queue): a high-performance asynchronous messaging library that provides a simple and flexible API for building distributed applications; supports various messaging patterns, including publish-subscribe, request-reply, and dealer-worker; often used in IIoT applications for its scalability and low latency (very flexible)
OPC-UA (Open Platform Communications, Unified Architecture): a platform-independent, service-oriented architecture for industrial automation; provides a standardized way to access and exchange data between devices and systems in industrial environments; supports both client-server and publish-subscribe communication models (industrial-grade, complex)

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Storage
A plant can produce a staggering amount of data, which can be difficult to manage and analyze without proper storage strategies

Cloud storage: scalable and flexible, but can have latency and security concerns
On-premises storage: more control and security, but can be costly and less scalable
Hybrid storage: combines the benefits of both cloud and on-premises storage, allowing for a more flexible and cost-effective solution







Important


Regardless of where data is stored, there are different storeage models to consider:

Data lakes: store raw, unstructured data in its native format, allowing for flexibility and scalability; can be more difficult to manage and analyze due to lack of structure
Data warehouses: store structured data in a predefined schema, optimized for querying and analysis; can be less flexible and scalable than data lakes
Data marts: store a subset of data from a data warehouse, focused on a specific business area or function; can provide faster access to relevant data, but can lead to data silos and inconsistencies

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Data lakes
Data lakes are designed to store raw, unstructured data in its native format. They can handle a wide variety of data types, including structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data
Shape of data can be freely changed, which leads to flexibility and scalability, but can make data management and analysis more difficult due to lack of structure
Commonly used technologies for data lakes include:

Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS): a distributed file system that provides scalable and fault-tolerant storage for large volumes of data; often used in conjunction with the Hadoop ecosystem for big data processing
Amazon S3: a cloud-based object storage service that provides scalable and durable storage
Azure Data Lake Storage: a cloud-based data lake service that provides scalable and secure storage for big data analytics
MongoDB: a NoSQL database that can be used as a data lake for storing unstructured data; provides flexibility, scalability, and can run on-premises, but may not be optimized for large-scale analytics. It stores data as collections of JSON documents

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Data warehouses
Data warehouses are designed to store structured data in a predefined schema, optimized for querying and analysis
This often relies on a relational database management system (RDBMS), which organizes data into tables with rows and columns, and allows for complex queries using SQL
Wrt data lakes, data warehouses can provide faster access to relevant data for analysis, but can be less flexible and scalable due to the need for predefined schemas and the overhead of managing structured data
As such, RDBMS-based data warehouses are often used for mature, well-defined data that is used for reporting and analysis, while data lakes are often used for raw, unstructured data that may require more exploration and processing before analysis

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
text:
Analysis
Some preliminary analysis and data manipulation can be performed on the data lake or data warehouse.
For example, MongoDB provides a powerful query language and aggregation (map-reduce actions) framework that can be used for data analysis and transformation (pipelines), allowing for some level of analysis to be performed directly on the data lake, without the need for a separate data warehouse
MongoDB pipelines can be set as read-only virtual tables, or views, that can be queried like regular tables, but the underlying data is not duplicated, and the view is updated in real-time as the underlying data changes

title: Distributed data acquisition and processing
section: Machine learning for anomaly detection and predictive maintenance
text:
Machine learning for anomaly detection and predictive maintenance
Machine learning models can be trained on the data stored in the data lake or data warehouse to later perform tasks such as anomaly detection and predictive maintenance, in real-time
Machine learning models can be supervised or unsupervised, can be implemented with conventional algorithms (e.g., SVM, PCA) or deep learning architectures (e.g., CNNs, RNNs), and can be deployed on the edge or in the cloud, depending on the requirements of the application
